  
**IoT \- Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis and Monitoring Device Using WSN for Home Appliances**

by  
**Rodwil James L. Domingo**  
**Christian E. Ladislao**  
**Eruel Joseph F. Manales**  
**Jarold Jan G. Silverio**  
**Janette Grace N. Siervo**

Bachelor of Science in Computer Engineering Eulogio “Amang” Rodriguez Institute of Science and Technology, 2026

**Introduction**

In modern households, electrical energy consumption continues to increase as more appliances and electronic devices become part of daily living. From basic necessities such as electric fans and refrigerators to entertainment and communication devices, the growing reliance on electricity has significantly contributed to higher overall energy demand. Despite this continuous rise, many household users remain unaware of how much electricity individual appliances consume, leading to inefficient energy usage and increased utility expenses.

Real-time monitoring and analysis of electrical consumption have therefore become important tools in promoting energy awareness and cost efficiency. By providing users with accurate information about their power usage, monitoring systems enable better decision-making and encourage responsible energy consumption. Over the years, various smart metering technologies and Internet of Things (IoT)-based solutions have been developed to address this need. These systems commonly use cloud platforms, mobile applications, and online databases to collect, process, and display energy data.

Although effective, many existing solutions depend heavily on continuous internet connectivity and advanced data infrastructures. Such requirements may increase system cost, complexity, and maintenance demands. Additionally, some commercially available systems involve invasive installation procedures or complicated wiring setups, which may not be suitable for ordinary household users seeking a simple and practical monitoring solution.

With the advancement of wireless sensor networks (WSN) and microcontroller technologies, new opportunities have emerged for developing affordable and efficient energy monitoring systems. These technologies make it possible to design standalone or offline-based devices capable of providing accurate real-time readings without relying entirely on cloud services.

However, there remains a gap in the availability of low-cost, user-friendly systems that can simultaneously monitor multiple household appliances while operating independently of constant internet access. Addressing this gap, the present study aims to develop an IoT-Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis and Monitoring Device using a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) that provides reliable, real-time energy monitoring in a simple and accessible setup suitable for home use.

**Research Design**

            This research will employ a developmental research design using a mixed-methods approach to create, develop, execute, and assess an IoT-Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis and  Monitoring Device Using WSN for Home Appliances. The system will utilize SCT-013 current sensors linked to an ESP32 microcontroller to monitor the electrical current used by specific household appliances and transform the gathered signals into quantifiable electrical metrics like current, estimated voltage, and computed power usage through standard electrical equations. Utilizing the Internet of Things (IoT) structure, the analyzed data will be sent through Wi-Fi to a web-based monitoring system, where it will be stored and shown on an online dashboard allowing users to track real-time electricity usage and examine past energy consumption data. The research will collect quantitative data such as electrical measurements, system response time, and data transmission reliability to analyze the accuracy, stability, and performance of the created device, whereas qualitative data will be obtained through structured observation and user feedback to evaluate the usability and effectiveness of the online monitoring interface. Data gathering will include regulated experimental trials, ongoing system monitoring, and benchmarking against standard electrical measurement tools to confirm the precision and dependability of the system. The study will take place in a regulated lab setting, where hardware assembly, sensor calibration, and system programming will occur, and in a genuine household environment, where the device will undergo testing with actual appliance loads and network conditions to confirm that the created IoT-based monitoring system is efficient, dependable, and appropriate for tracking home energy usage in practical scenarios.

**List of Materials**

| Components | Quantity | Image | Description | Application |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | ----- |
| **ESP32-DEV-38P (Micro Controller)** | **1** | **![][image1]** | ESP32 is a microcontroller with built-in Wi-Fi that processes sensor data in IoT systems. | It receives voltage and current data from the sensors, calculates the power consumption, and sends the information to the web-based monitoring system through the internet. |
| **SCT \- 013 \- 000 sensors** | **3** | **![][image2]** | It is a non-invasive current transformer sensor that is used to sense the AC current without necessarily being a part of the circuit.  | Applied to AC power systems to detect the current consumption of domestic appliances.  |
| **Burden Resistors** | **3** | **\-** | Resistors used to convert current signals to output voltage in a safe manner using current transformers. | They are used in the circuit of current transformers in order to convert the input current signals into measurable voltage that the microcontrollers can interpret. |
| **Breadboard** | **1-2** | **![][image3]** | A prototyping board which can be reused and allows connecting of components and wires without soldering.  | It was used in simple testing and assembly of circuits without soldering.  |
| **Jumper Wires** | **2 set**  | **![][image4]** | Flexible insulated wires which are utilized to make fast electrical connections in bread boards, and modules. | These were prototyped to interconnect components, sensors and modules. |
| **Soldering Iron** | **1**  | **![][image5]** | Soldering Iron is a handheld electronic tool used to heat and melt solder to join electronic components and wires on a circuit board. | heats the Solder Wire (Lead) until it melts and flows between electronic components and PCB pads to create a secure electrical connection. |
| **Solder Wire (Lead)** | **1 set** | **![][image6]** | Solder Wire (Lead) is a metal alloy wire used to permanently connect electronic components by melting and solidifying between conductive surfaces. | melts when heated by a Soldering Iron, flowing onto metal contacts and solidifying to form a strong electrical and mechanical bond. |
| **Rechargeable Battery** | **2** | **![][image7]** | A portable power source that stores electrical energy and supplies power to electronic devices.  | It provides backup or portable power to the monitoring device, allowing the ESP32 and sensors to continue operating even when the main power supply is unavailable.  |

**Statement of the Problem**  
This study aims to design and develop an IoT-based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis and Monitoring Device using Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) to efficiently measure, transmit, store, and display real-time energy usage of multiple home appliances through a web-based platform.

Specifically, this study aims to:

1. **Design** a non-invasive current sensing circuit using SCT-013 sensors that can safely detect and measure the power consumption of selected household appliances.  
2. **Construct** multiple wireless sensor nodes using ESP32 microcontrollers that process energy consumption data and transmit it via WiFi.  
3. **Develop** a cloud-based centralized monitoring system with a web dashboard that stores, manages, and displays real-time and historical energy consumption data from all sensor nodes.  
4. **Create** a microcontroller program that enables accurate data acquisition, processing, wireless transmission, and configurable threshold-based notifications.  
5. **Design** the IoT system architecture and data flow that manage how energy consumption data is measured, transmitted over a wireless network, stored in the cloud, and accessed through a web interface.  
   

**Keywords:** Current Sensing Circuit, Sensor Nodes, Central Monitoring Device, Program for Microcontrollers, IoT system and Data flow

**Research Setting**  
The study was conducted in a residential household environment where common home appliances such as electric fans, televisions, and refrigerators are typically used. The researchers developed and tested the proposed IoT-based integrated power consumption monitoring device using a local Wi-Fi network to simulate a real home setup. The system utilized an ESP32-DevKit microcontroller as the central controller due to its built-in Wi-Fi capability, enabling wireless communication and real-time data transmission.

Current consumption from the selected appliances was measured using SCT-013-000 non-invasive current transformer sensors, which detect electrical current without directly interrupting the circuit. The signals collected from the sensors were processed through burden resistors to convert the current signal into a measurable voltage suitable for the ESP32 microcontroller. All electronic components were initially assembled and tested on a breadboard to allow flexible prototyping and easy circuit modification during the development phase.

The ESP32 transmitted the collected power consumption data through the household Wi-Fi network to a web-based monitoring platform, where the information could be accessed and analyzed in real time. This setup allowed the researchers to simulate how homeowners could remotely monitor and analyze the energy consumption of their appliances using an internet-connected device. The research setting ensured that the system operates under typical residential conditions, providing realistic data for evaluating the effectiveness and functionality of the proposed monitoring device.

**Respondents of the Study**  
The respondents of the study consisted of the researchers and selected household occupants where the IoT-Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis system was implemented and tested. The population of the study was limited to a residential household environment where the monitoring system could be installed and evaluated under actual operating conditions. From this population, a small sample group composed of the researchers and household members participated in the evaluation of the developed system.

The researchers served as the technical evaluators of the system. They were responsible for installing the sensing device, configuring the ESP microcontroller, verifying the current readings obtained from the SCT-013 sensor, and monitoring the reliability of data transmission to the cloud-based database and web-based monitoring interface. Their technical background enabled them to assess the functionality, accuracy, and consistency of the developed monitoring system.

The household respondents were the occupants of the residence where the system was deployed. They accessed the web-based monitoring platform to observe the electrical consumption data generated by the system. Their interaction with the monitoring interface allowed the researchers to evaluate the system’s usability, accessibility, and effectiveness in presenting energy consumption information in a residential setting.

The study utilized a non-probability sampling technique, specifically purposive sampling, since the household and participants were intentionally selected based on their ability to accommodate the installation and testing of the monitoring system and their relevance to the objectives of the research.

**Data Gathering Instruments**  
The primary instrument used in the study was the developed IoT-Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis system. The system consisted of the SCT-013 current sensor used to measure electrical current from household electrical loads and the ESP microcontroller which processed the sensor readings and transmitted the collected data through a wireless internet connection. The processed data were then sent to a cloud-based database where the information was stored and organized.

A web-based monitoring interface served as the platform where the collected data were displayed and analyzed. Through this interface, the electrical consumption readings could be viewed and monitored in real time as well as recorded for future comparison and analysis. The monitoring system functioned as the main instrument for collecting and storing electrical consumption data necessary for the evaluation of the system’s performance.

**Data Gathering Procedures**  
The study followed a case study approach in collecting the necessary data since the developed monitoring system was implemented within a specific residential environment. The researchers first installed the SCT-013 current sensor on selected electrical lines of household appliances and connected it to the ESP microcontroller for signal acquisition and processing.

After the installation process, the system was configured to transmit the collected electrical current readings through Wi-Fi to the cloud-based database. The transmitted data were then displayed on the web-based monitoring interface, allowing the researchers to observe the real-time electrical consumption of the monitored appliances.

An experimental method was applied during the testing phase of the system. The researchers monitored the sensor readings and evaluated the behavior of the monitoring system while it was operating under actual household electrical loads. During this phase, participant observation was conducted by observing the stability of the sensor measurements, the reliability of the data transmission, and the responsiveness of the web-based monitoring interface.

The household occupants were allowed to access the monitoring interface to view the recorded power consumption data. Their interaction with the system enabled the researchers to observe how the monitoring platform presented information to users in a practical residential environment. The collected system data were then compiled and prepared for statistical analysis.

**Statistical Treatment**  
Statistical tools were utilized in the study to analyze and interpret the collected data and to determine whether the hypotheses of the research should be accepted or rejected. Statistical treatment was applied to provide meaningful interpretation of the electrical measurements obtained from the developed monitoring system.

Descriptive statistical methods such as the mean and standard deviation were used to analyze the recorded sensor readings and evaluate the consistency of the collected data. The mean was used to determine the average value of the electrical measurements obtained during system operation, while the standard deviation was used to determine the variability and reliability of the recorded data.

In addition, percentage error was applied to evaluate the accuracy of the developed monitoring system by comparing the obtained sensor readings with reference measurements. These statistical treatments enabled the researchers to interpret the collected data and assess the reliability, accuracy, and effectiveness of the IoT-Based Integrated Power Consumption Analysis system.

*Fig 1\. Conceptual Framework*

              *Fig 2\. Block Diagram*  
**![][image9]**  
*Fig 3\. Flowchart*

**IPO Framework**

| INPUT | PROCESS | OUTPUT |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Electrical Signals Electric Current (A)** – Alternating current measured using the SCT-013-000 non-invasive current sensor attached to appliance power lines. **Mains Voltage (V)** – Standard AC household voltage used as a reference value for estimating electrical power consumption. **Household Power Frequency (Hz)** – Typical residential electrical frequency used during current measurement and RMS computation. **Hardware Components** ESP32 microcontroller for data acquisition, processing, and WiFi communication SCT-013-000 current sensor for non-invasive current detection Burden resistor and signal conditioning circuit for converting current to measurable voltage Battery power supply for sensor node operation **Software and System Requirements** Arduino IDE for programming the ESP32 ESP libraries for sensor reading and WiFi communication Web development tools (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) Cloud database platform for data storage **Calibration Standards** Reference measurements obtained from a digital clamp meter or multimeter used to validate sensor accuracy. | System Design and Component Identification Selection of required hardware and software components Design of system architecture and block diagram Identification of data flow between sensor node, cloud server, and web dashboard Sensor Node Development Construction of current sensing circuit using SCT-013 sensor and burden resistor Integration of the sensor with the ESP32 microcontroller Implementation of RMS current measurement and signal calibration Central System Integration Configuration of ESP32 WiFi communication Transmission of processed sensor data to a cloud database Establishment of communication between hardware and web monitoring platform Program Development Development of firmware for ESP32 data acquisition and processing Implementation of power computation using the formula **P \= V × I** Formatting and transmitting data to the cloud server Development of web dashboard for real-time monitoring and visualization System Testing and Evaluation Verification of WiFi data transmission and system connectivity Comparison of sensor readings with standard measuring instruments Evaluation of system accuracy using statistical measures such as mean, standard deviation, and percentage error Implementation of system adjustments and improvements | **Functional IoT-Based Monitoring System Real-time monitoring of appliance current and estimated power consumption Wireless data transmission through WiFi Cloud-based storage of energy consumption data Web-based monitoring dashboard displaying:** Real-time readings Historical consumption data Energy usage visualization through graphs **Analyzed Power Consumption Data** Processed electrical consumption readings Accuracy evaluation results Percentage error analysis **Operational Prototype** A functional IoT-based power monitoring device capable of tracking energy consumption of selected home appliances.  |

***Phase 1: System Design and Component Identification (Input Phase)***  
Step 1: Identification of Hardware Components  
The researchers identified the hardware components required to build the proposed monitoring system. These included the ESP32 microcontroller, SCT-013-000 non-invasive current sensor, burden resistors for signal conditioning, battery power supply, and necessary electronic components such as resistors, jumper wires, and circuit boards. The specifications of each component, including voltage ratings, current limits, and sensor sensitivity, were reviewed to ensure compatibility, safety, and reliable operation of the system.

Step 2: Identification of Software Requirements  
The software tools required for the development of the system were identified. These included the Arduino IDE for programming the ESP32 microcontroller, necessary ESP libraries for analog data acquisition and WiFi communication, and web development tools such as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for building the web-based monitoring interface. A cloud database platform was also selected to store and manage the transmitted energy consumption data.

Step 3: Selection of Appliances for Testing  
Several household appliances were selected to serve as test loads for the monitoring system. These included commonly used appliances such as an electric fan, rice cooker, and television. The selected appliances represent different levels of power consumption, allowing the researchers to evaluate the system's ability to monitor and analyze varying energy usage patterns in a typical household environment.

Step 4: Initial System Architecture Design  
The researchers developed the initial system architecture through a block diagram illustrating the overall operation of the system. The design shows the process of current sensing from the appliance using the SCT-013 sensor, signal processing through the ESP32 microcontroller, wireless transmission of data through WiFi, storage of the data in a cloud database, and visualization of the energy consumption data through a web-based monitoring dashboard.

***Phase 2: Sensor Node Development  (Process)***  
Step 5: Construct the non-invasive current sensing circuit using SCT-013 sensors and burden resistors. 

The development of the sensor node begins with the construction of a non-invasive current sensing circuit using the SCT-013 current transformer sensor and a burden resistor. The SCT-013 sensor is clamped around the live conductor of the electrical line to detect the current flowing through the load without direct electrical contact. Because the sensor produces a current output, a burden resistor is used to convert this current signal into a corresponding voltage signal that can be measured by the microcontroller’s analog input.

Step 6: Integrate each sensor with an ESP32 microcontroller for analog data acquisition.

After constructing the sensing circuit, the sensor output is connected to the analog input pin of the ESP32 microcontroller. The ESP32 serves as the data acquisition unit of the sensor node, where the voltage signal generated by the sensor is continuously sampled through the built-in analog-to-digital converter. This allows the microcontroller to capture real-time electrical current data from the monitored electrical load.

Step 7: Implement signal calibration and RMS current computation within the ESP32.

Once the sensor readings are acquired, signal calibration is implemented within the ESP32 to ensure that the measured values correspond accurately to the actual electrical current. The microcontroller processes the sampled analog signals and performs Root Mean Square (RMS) current computation to determine the effective current value of the alternating current waveform. The computed RMS value represents the usable current measurement required for analyzing electrical power consumption.

Step 8: Verify sensor accuracy by comparing readings with standard measurement tools.  
Sensor accuracy is then evaluated by comparing the readings obtained from the developed sensor node with measurements taken from standard electrical measuring instruments such as a clamp meter or multimeter. This comparison allows the researchers to determine the reliability and precision of the sensing circuit before the collected data is transmitted to the cloud-based monitoring platform for further monitoring and analysis.

***Phase 3: Central Monitoring Integration***  
Step 9: Development of the Cloud-Based Monitoring Platform

In this step, the researchers developed a cloud-based monitoring platform that serves as the central system for collecting and displaying power consumption data. A web-based dashboard was created to receive the transmitted data from the sensor nodes through the internet. The platform was designed to store, process, and visualize the electrical measurements such as current and estimated power consumption. This allowed users to monitor appliance energy usage remotely through a web interface.

Step 10: Configuration of Wireless and Internet Communication

During this step, the researchers configured the communication process that enables sensor nodes to send collected data to the cloud server. The microcontroller connected to the sensor node was programmed to transmit processed sensor readings through the wireless network and internet connection. Communication parameters such as device identification, transmission interval, and data formatting were defined to ensure proper delivery of information to the cloud database. This configuration ensured stable and continuous data transmission from the sensor nodes to the web-based monitoring platform.

Step 11: Integration of Sensor Nodes with the Web-Based Dashboard

In this step, the researchers integrated all sensor nodes with the cloud-based monitoring dashboard. The incoming data from each node was identified, decoded, and displayed on the web interface in real time. The dashboard was designed to organize the data according to the corresponding appliance being monitored. Through this integration, users were able to view real-time energy consumption information remotely using computers or mobile devices connected to the internet.

***Phase 4: Program Development (Process)***  
Step 12: Creating and sending the firmware to the ESP32 microcontroller.  
The researchers developed the firmware using the Arduino IDE to program the ESP32-DEV-38P microcontroller. The code allows the system to read current signals from the SCT-013-000 non-invasive current sensor, process the signals, and prepare the data for wireless transmission.

Step 13: Calculating the electrical parameters and how much energy is being used.  
The program processes the collected sensor data to calculate electrical parameters such as current, voltage reference, and power consumption using the formula P \= V × I. These calculations enable the system to estimate the real-time energy consumption of connected household appliances.

Step 14: Connecting Wireless Transmission and Web-Based Monitoring Together  
The ESP32 sends the processed power consumption data via Wi-Fi to a cloud database and a web-based monitoring dashboard. The web system shows live electricity usage from appliances, how much power they use, and past energy consumption data so you can keep track and understand your usage.

***Phase 5: Optimization and Testing of the system (Output Phase)***  
​Step 15: Evaluation of System Performance  
​        The researchers conduct intensive testing in order to test the efficiency of the working system. This involves checking the accuracy of the measurements of the SCT-013 sensor, ESP32 microcontroller with the common power readings to ascertain data accuracy. Also, the researchers investigate the stability of WiFi communication as a means of transferring data and reactiveness of the web-based monitoring dashboard so that the user can receive real-time updates.  
​  
Step 16: Analysis of Abnormal Consumption Detection  
​        The researchers examine the functionality of abnormal consumption detection capabilities of the system. The researchers assess the abilities of the system to detect and indicate abnormal energy consumption that does not correspond to the common house patterns through the tracking of the power patterns of the chosen appliances (the electric fan or rice cooker).  
​  
Step 17: Final System Optimization  
​        The researchers make final optimization on the hardware, firmware and cloud parameters of the system. This shall incorporate the process of optimizing the software code and hardware interrelations so that it can be stable to allow continuous monitoring and also address any problems that are detected during the initial testing of the device and then finalize the integrated power consumption analysis device.

**MATRIX OF METHODS**

| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Phase 1: System Design and Component Identification | The phase was conducted in the researchers’ home workspace and small laboratory area where system planning, component selection, and system architecture design were performed. | The sources of technical data included system components such as the ESP32 microcontroller, SCT-013-000, burden resistors, battery supply, and related electronic components required for the IoT-based monitoring system. Technical information was also obtained from manufacturer datasheets and engineering references. | The researchers reviewed component datasheets, examined manufacturer specifications, and gathered technical information from reliable engineering resources. Selected household appliances were also inspected to determine their suitability for testing the monitoring system. | Component datasheets, manufacturer specification sheets, online engineering references, schematic design tools, and system architecture diagrams used during the system design stage. | Descriptive analysis was used to evaluate the specifications and compatibility of the selected components to ensure accurate sensing, stable communication, and proper system operation. |
| Phase 2: Sensor Node Developement | The activities in this phase were conducted at the researchers’ home or workspace where the sensing circuit and microcontroller system were assembled and tested. Standard household electrical lines and appliances served as the source of current measurements to simulate real operating conditions. | Research themselves, as they construct the circuits, programmed the Arduino Nano and Configure the wireless module. | 1\. Construction of SCT-013 and connecting it to Arduino Nano. 2\. Bench testing to verify signals and wiring. 3\. Install  firmware and monitor its output via serial  Monitoring. 4\. Testing of wireless communication to ensure its stability, signal strength as well the delays. 5\. Check measurements to appliances under different loads. 6\. Calibration and firmware adjustment should be made until steady values are achieved.   | SCT-013 non-invasive current sensors, Arduino Nano microcontroller, serial monitor of Arduino IDE, calibrated digital clamp meter, NRF24L01 wireless module diagnostics. | 1\. Mean \- to calculate representative sensor output.2\. Standard deviation \- to determine consistency.3\. Percentage error \- compare the sensor data with reference instruments to determine the error. |
| Phase 3: Central System Integration | The integration and testing were conducted in the researchers’ home workspace and small laboratory area equipped with stable electrical outlets, internet connectivity, and computer systems for accessing the cloud dashboard. The environment allowed the researchers to configure the wireless communication, monitor real-time data transmission, and evaluate the performance of the web-based monitoring platform.  | The respondents in this phase were the developed system components including the sensor nodes, wireless communication modules, and the cloud-based monitoring platform. The researchers served as system operators responsible for configuring the network, integrating the devices, and validating the data transmission to the cloud dashboard.  | 1\. Set up the cloud-based monitoring dashboard for receiving sensor data. 2\. Configure the sensor nodes to transmit processed current readings through the wireless network and internet connection. 3\. Establish communication between the sensor nodes and the cloud server. 4\. Monitor the incoming data displayed on the web-based dashboard. 5\. Perform multiple data transmission tests to evaluate stability and accuracy. 6\. Record and analyze the transmitted data from different appliances.  | SCT-013 current sensors, Arduino Nano microcontrollers, Wi-Fi internet connection, cloud-based dashboard platform, personal computer or laptop for accessing the web interface, serial monitor tools, and reference measuring  | 1\. Mean – to determine the average transmitted sensor readings. 2\. Standard Deviation –to measure the consistency of the transmitted data. 3\. Percentage Error – to compare the system readings with reference measurement instruments for accuracy evaluation.  |
| Phase 4: Program Development | Conducted in the researchers' home workspace and laboratory area where stable power supply and internet access were available for program development and testing of the IoT-based power monitoring device. | Household users served as respondents for evaluating the usability and functionality of the developed web-based monitoring system. | 1\. Assemble the hardware components including the ESP32-DEV-38P microcontroller, SCT-013-000 current sensors, burden resistors, breadboard, and jumper wires. 2\. Program the ESP32 microcontroller to read current data from the SCT-013-000 sensors. 3\. Configure Wi-Fi connectivity for the ESP32 to transmit collected data. 4\. Send the sensor readings to the web-based monitoring platform through the internet. 5\. Store and process the data in the database of the web system. 6\. Display the real-time power consumption of connected appliances on the web interface. 7\. Perform system testing and debugging to ensure accurate data transmission and monitoring. 8\. Allow household users to access the web interface and evaluate the system functionality. | ESP32-DEV-38P microcontroller, SCT-013-000 current sensors, burden resistors, breadboard, jumper wires, laptop/computer for programming, Wi-Fi internet connection, web-based monitoring system, and evaluation questionnaire. | 1\. Mean – to determine the average evaluation of the system performance. 2\. Standard Deviation – to measure the consistency of the responses. 3\. Percentage – to compare the gathered evaluation results. |
| Phase 5: System Testing, Evaluation, and Enhancement  | Researchers’ home workspace and small laboratory area | The key sources of data are the selected household appliances (electric fan, rice cooker, and television) and system components such as the ESP32 and SCT-013 sensor. | The researchers follow a validation methodology that includes comparing system readings to a calibrated meter, simulating abnormal loads, and monitoring cloud data transmission stability. | The researchers use a digital power meter for baseline data, an SCT-013 current sensor, and a web-based monitoring dashboard to log performance  | Descriptive analysis is performed to assess WiFi reliability and dashboard responsiveness. |
